summary: Martin Fowler argues that IT projects fall into two distinct categories—utility and strategic—requiring fundamentally different management approaches. Utility projects like payroll should 'just work' with minimal cost and customization, while strategic projects enable competitive differentiation and demand rapid iteration. The key distinction is whether the underlying business function is a differentiator. Fowler critiques the popular 'Bimodal IT' concept as a flawed interpretation that wrongly separates systems by technical layer rather than business value, and falsely assumes speed requires accepting defects.
key_points: The utility vs strategic distinction is about the business function, not the technology—strategic functions differentiate you from competitors
For utility projects, buy packages and adapt your process to match; for strategic projects, build custom software to enable differentiation
Risk profiles differ fundamentally: utility projects fear catastrophic failure, strategic projects fear being outpaced by competitors
The 80/20 rule applies—perhaps 95/5—meaning very few projects are truly strategic, but organizations often misclassify too many as strategic
Bimodal IT is a flawed model because it separates by technical layer instead of business value, and falsely trades quality for speed
money_quotes: If how you do this function is a crucial part of what makes you better than the competition, then the software that supports this function needs to be as good as you can make it.
For strategic projects, the biggest risk is not doing something before your competitors do.
For a strategic function you don't want the same software as your competitors because that would cripple your ability to differentiate.
It's like expecting the same people who design warehouses to design an arts museum.
High quality (and low defects) are a crucial enabler for rapid cycle-time.
'Shades of grey' give license to pile things into the wrong category; things that are really utility will be given an inflated importance.
key_insight: The fundamental question for any IT initiative isn't about the technology itself, but whether the underlying business function differentiates you from competitors—and this distinction should drive radically different approaches to staffing, budgeting, and methodology.
